food_name: Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton)
aliases: Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton)
region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China — a ubiquitous hawker and restaurant starter in Singapore
food_subgroup: Deep-fried thin wheat flour wonton wrapper with pork and prawn filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Fried Wonton. Closest parent is a deep-fried thin wheat flour pastry with meat filling. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried thin wheat flour wrapper with pork, prawn, and water chestnut filling — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for fried wonton; A0CEB (deep-fried pastry with meat filling) used as closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 100g, equivalent to 6–7 individual fried wontons.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Wonton (Deep-fried Pork and Prawn Wonton). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this exact match. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this item; these fields are set to -1 to indicate not reported by source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat-based deep-fried pastry products — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried wonton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is estimated from the closest analogous food: thin wheat flour wrapper products (GI range ~65–75 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat-based pastry and dumpling wrappers). Deep-frying fat content substantially slows gastric emptying and suppresses the effective GI of the wheat wrapper. GI of 68 (medium category) is a defensible midpoint estimate. GL per 100g serving = (68 × 22.5) / 100 = 15.3; GL per 80g portion = 12.2 as reported.

hypertension.guidance: Each 100g serving of fried wonton delivers 480 mg sodium from soy sauce seasoning in the filling; a typical 80g portion (approximately 5–6 pieces) contributes 384 mg sodium, and adding a standard 15g sweet chilli dipping sauce raises the total to approximately 500 mg — representing 22% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals, so portion control and avoidance of high-sodium dipping sauces are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 68 (medium), carbohydrate 22.5 g per 100g, GL 12.2 per 80g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried wonton has a medium GI of 68; the high fat content from deep-frying moderates postprandial glucose response, and the protein from pork and prawn provides additional glycaemic moderation, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 80g (approximately 5–6 pieces) to keep glycaemic load at approximately 12 and avoid pairing with high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 16.5 g per 100g; saturated fat 3.5 g per 100g; cholesterol 55 mg per 100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried wonton provides 16.5 g total fat and 3.5 g saturated fat per 100g from deep-frying oil and pork filling; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and choose lower-fat cooking methods or boiled wonton alternatives to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Wontons (炸云吞 / 炸雲吞, Wonton Goreng) are one of Singapore's most popular Cantonese-origin Chinese appetisers and hawker snacks — thin, golden, shatteringly crispy triangular or crescent-shaped parcels made from ultra-thin wheat flour wonton wrappers folded around a seasoned filling of minced pork and chopped prawn, then deep-fried in hot oil until the wrapper puffs, blisters, and turns a deep amber gold. The delicate wrapper creates a light, airy crunch on the outside while the filling inside remains tender and juicy from the pork fat and prawn moisture. Seasonings typically include light soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, ginger, and spring onion. They are served with sweet chilli sauce, plum sauce, or Worcestershire-soy dipping sauce. Per 100g, fried wontons provide 285 kcal, 12.5g protein, 16.5g fat (3.5g saturated), and 22.5g carbohydrate with 480mg sodium. The GI is estimated at 68 (medium), with a glycaemic load of approximately 12 per 80g serving. Unlike boiled wontons in wonton noodle soup, the fried version has substantially more fat from the cooking oil and a more intense, caramelised flavour from the Maillard reaction. Clinically, the sodium content warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, and the fat content is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Allergens include gluten (wheat wrapper) and crustaceans (prawn).
common_user_queries: fried wonton vs boiled wonton calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-based deep-fried pastry products; no direct GI measurement for fried wonton exists in the database. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by the HPB source and are set to -1.
notes: Fried Wontons are a classic Cantonese-origin appetiser widely consumed across Singapore hawker centres, Chinese restaurants, and dim sum establishments. The filling typically combines minced pork and prawn with soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, and spring onion. The thin wheat flour wrapper is folded into triangles or crescents and deep-fried until crispy. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this item and are recorded as -1 (not reported by source). The halal status is marked false as pork is a primary ingredient. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for fried wonton is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
